history: The client has a history of a past labor dispute in a previous workplace, which likely contributes to their current heightened sensitivity to fairness and authority issues. They have ongoing concerns related to fairness, pay, and authority at work. The emotional impact of previous negative experiences appears to intensify their current reactions to workplace demands, especially those perceived as unjust.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
intermediate_belief: If I don't stand up for myself, others will take advantage of me. Authority figures can't be trusted to treat me fairly. I must be vigilant or I will be exploited.
intermediate_belief_depression: If nothing changes, I will always be stuck in unfair situations and nothing I do will make a difference.
coping_strategies: The client tends to avoid direct confrontation with authority figures and may withdraw from workplace interactions. They seek validation and support from colleagues who share similar concerns, but these discussions often focus on grievances, reinforcing negative emotions. The client may also comply passively or engage in minimal resistance, such as procrastination.
situation: Being asked to work beyond regular hours without additional compensation during an ongoing labor dispute.
auto_thought: This is so unfair—I'm being taken advantage of again. No matter what I do, they won't respect my rights.
behavior: I avoid speaking up directly to my manager. I complain to coworkers about the unfairness. I do the extra work but feel resentful and disengaged.
